And also if you can without giving too much away, is it only strong by weight or by volume as well? I was just considering a similar method for making some molded frames for something I'm making at home and wanted this information before buying all the material.

author: @dalemyers9265
text: i could contest the variations in the two types of plastics and the thickness of each. as a thicker harder object can dissipate more heat. as the thinner less dense plastic can't and wont. but that to the side. being graphene is a product of graphite. graphite being a product of diamonds. it can withstand heat and pressure. i like how it looks to char. i can see it being utilized in a dragon-skin type of ballistic defeating. armor. based on the over lying design of lizard scales. there is a web site for said products. but it uses metalized-ceramics the last i heard. but ceramics don't perform as well on second impact..and being it withstands heat from flames as it does. it may have a second use in firefighting armor too. when a fire fighters enters a militant zone or even in a bombed out area with incendiary bombs and shards of masonry begin to pop. sending fragmenting bricks flying about.. just saying.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +dale myers Thanks for that mate – interesting ideas. I wouldn't worry too much about the thickness issue. We all know how PP reacts. The only thing a thicker bit in a vice would do is take slightly longer to burn – and that would be slight. You have to remember this isn't a scientific test – rather a youtube demo the music should have been a hint at that

Robert Murray-Smith
I see that. But are you going to preform scientific tests for us at some point, or are we stuck with only seeing it be shot at and lit with a torch? I'd like to see real data. This shouldn't be like the Discovery Channel where anything scientific must be dumbed down and depicted in a cartoon format so a 6 year old child could understand things.

Hi Robert, 
Your videos are awesome and full of potential ideas for the futhure reserch! Right now I am starting student in material science field. So I wondered is there any chance for me to get information from you about polimer you used to make this plastic wonder or/and producing tehnickues you were used to make such an unique thing.

author: @JohnnyForehead
text:
If you can't create any significant commercial interest Robert then maybe a  Kickstarter campaign or some other crowd-funded campaign  might be the holy grail. I don't think it has been done yet. I mean, the release of proprietary chemical  trade secrets via crowd-funding. You could probably get a shit load of publicity off that alone. 

Set your price, then get us minions to push it. Apart from selling the incredible material properties and listing a host of uses for it, you could sell the concept of the infinite directions and benefits that will derive mankind in this instance and future instances if open sourced ideas "big ideas" and inventions aren't locked up.

You also don't get bogged down in business and licensing which has the habit of sucking the original creative drive and passion from the person who's interests often don't lie in that direction.

I'd sure as hell would push the shit out of it to get you your desired goal and would be one of your first backers. A few million dollars I think would be a reasonable outcome, which would help fund your future endeavors.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
+JohnnyForehead 

Hiya mate – that is really interesting. I can tell you I like it – a lot. I can't
see how the perks would relate to the campaign. Supporters would have
to get something out of it for a return of their support but I am not
sure how to arrange that within the kickstart framework – but it
did cause me and Steve to stop and think about it. If I can come up
with a way of doing that I will probably do as you suggest.

replies.author: @JohnnyForehead
replies.text:
+Robert Murray-Smith Some of the perks could be first access for a limited time period to the methodology. A kit of materials and a comprehensive video explaining how to make it.

 It's a do it yourself, maker world now, and the idea and concept alone I think would garner great support, just for a  T-shirt. My gut feeling is, if done right, this could be very successful. I'm really excited that your considering it, so I'm going to give it some serious consideration and research to hash out some potential pitfalls to balance my enthusiasm. I'll get back to ya.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +JohnnyForehead cheers mate - my email is robertmurraysmith64@gmail.com if you want to write directly - i like the t-shirt idea as a perk nice one

author: @actudoran
text:
lol great test mate!
had an ideea of melting a 3d print fillament and mix it with graphene ro make it uber strong ... the first thought was ... set the darn thing on fire :)

had a hunch that the graphene will conduct heat faster than plastic hence creating micropockets self insulating by vaporising the surrounding plastic ... 
interestingly enough ... if heated below the melting point of the unaugmented plastic ... hhhhmmmm i wonder if it still insulates ... or by what measure ... 

so if you were to heat it to say 100 C .... what would it read on the other side ?  but maybe not because then the read on the cold side would have been larger considerably then the ambiental temp....

did you test it for magnetic permeability ? ... do magnets stick to it ?

awesome!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +actudoran it's not magnetic mate but around 100C it just becomes pliable again

replies.author: @actudoran
replies.text:
Interesting... :)

So no heatshield then ... :)

author: @DRandomD
text: Also is there a simple test to check the quality of graphene after making some?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +DRandomD not really mate - raman is the easiest

author: @DRandomD
text: Is the heat being transferred to the vice? The thermo properties of graphene might have implications for whatever it becomes attached to..

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +DRandomD given the other side is only 16 degrees C and 7mm away - what do you think mate?

replies.author: @DRandomD
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith that's impressive, I missed his first heat measurement the first time I saw this...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +DRandomD it could be the vice is a heat sink - but i doubt it mate because of the heat difference between the two sides - you would expect the sides to be relatively equal if the heat was being transferred that quickly

replies.author: @DRandomD
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith I thought perhaps it might be being transferred somewhere, in the way manjo bhargava hopes to harvest energy, but I guess if the graphine is not forming strands in the plastic this doesn't happen, would be interesting to look at the plastic under a big microscope to see how the graphene is arranged.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +DRandomD yes it would indeed

author: @dash-4150
text: Absolutely fenominal, the thermal dynamics are amazing, i would like to see test done on thinner sampls, so as to see its use for stove pipes.  Or stove pipe coatings to aid in heat transfer

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +joe carrigan interesting idea - i may look at it if i get time mate

author: @serfer5rogers
text: conducts heat and electricity, wow not bad, so many uses, i suspect from the temperature difference from the flame and the test piece that the vice maybe acting as a heat sink.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +serfer5rogers maybe - but you would expect the other side to get hot too - that was quite a temperature difference for 7mm of thickness

replies.author: @serfer5rogers
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith absolutely

replies.author: @eeezygoing
replies.text:
+serfer5rogers  Good observation serfer5rogers. Like you I suspect that the heat energy travels easier along the Graphene plane and down to the vice  rather than over the layers and to the other side. The test could be done again but this time by insulating the vice jaws from the sample using some thick plywood or just some wood or other heat insulative material. Otherwise you will have to market the material and thick vice jaws at the same time lol
Cheers

replies.author: @moto2kx2

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +moto2kx2 i am sorry if i came across a bit 'snotty' mate - i equally didn't mean it that way - I try to make this fun - so science is a bit more approachable. That can come across as a bit glib. It's a difficult balancing act and i get it wrong quite a lot - but still i keep trying lol - I take on board what you are saying but i tend do that kind of stuff a bit more behind the scenes so to speak - then i try and think how to demo the idea in a fun way.
